statement: I only read news that agrees with what I already believe — the rest feels untrustworthy.
explanation: Sources get dismissed for disagreeing, not because they're wrong.
reflectionQuestion: When did you last listen to a view you disagreed with — on purpose?
whyHumansDoThis: We lean toward what fits what we already think. It feels safer and easier — but it can narrow what we're willing to hear.
learningObjective: Learn that seeking confirming evidence feels natural but can limit better decisions.

statement: After I bought a stock, I only followed analysts who said it would rise — and ignored warnings about debt.
explanation: After deciding, only the good news gets noticed.
reflectionQuestion: After you decide something, do you look for doubts — or proof you were right?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we've committed, we want to feel we made the right call. Doubt is uncomfortable — so we often skip it.
learningObjective: Learn to actively look for disconfirming evidence after making decisions, not just before.

statement: My feed is full of posts I agree with — so it feels like everyone thinks the same way.
explanation: A curated feed gets mistaken for how most people think.
reflectionQuestion: Does your feed challenge you, or mostly agree with you?
whyHumansDoThis: We engage more with what we agree with. Platforms show us more of it — and the loop can feel like reality.
learningObjective: Learn how confirmation bias interacts with algorithms to create feedback loops that feel like reality.

statement: A house is listed at $500k. Even if that's high, buyers still haggle around that number.
explanation: The listing price becomes the anchor for every offer.
reflectionQuestion: Has a first price ever shaped what felt 'fair' to you?
whyHumansDoThis: The first number we see sticks. We adjust from there — but rarely far enough.
learningObjective: Learn to recognize when a starting number is shaping your judgment instead of the actual value.

statement: A jacket was $300, now $150 — feels like a steal, even if $150 is still a lot.
explanation: The old price makes the discount feel bigger than it is.
reflectionQuestion: Next 'sale' tag you see — what's the item actually worth?
whyHumansDoThis: We focus on the drop, not the real value. The starting price quietly pulls our judgment.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate the actual value of something rather than the discount from an arbitrary starting price.

statement: In a job interview, they ask for my salary first — and that number ends up anchoring the whole negotiation.
explanation: The first number spoken becomes the reference for everything after.
reflectionQuestion: Have you ever felt stuck near a number you said early on?
whyHumansDoThis: Early numbers are hard to let go of — even when new info suggests we should move on.
learningObjective: Learn that anchors set early in a conversation can quietly control the entire negotiation.

statement: After a few plane-crash stories in the news, flying feels terrifying — even though stats say driving is riskier.
explanation: Vivid stories feel more common than they are.
reflectionQuestion: Any fear you have that might come from memorable stories, not stats?
whyHumansDoThis: Easy-to-recall events feel more likely. Our brain uses memory as a shortcut.
learningObjective: Learn that memorable events are not always common events.

statement: I saw one viral post about a rare vaccine side effect — and it stuck more than the disease risks I ignored.
explanation: One vivid story outweighed the broader data.
reflectionQuestion: On health risks, do you check the data or trust the story that sticks?
whyHumansDoThis: Emotional stories lodge in memory. Dry statistics often don't.
learningObjective: Learn to seek base-rate statistics rather than relying on memorable anecdotes.

statement: After a friend's house was burgled, burglaries suddenly feel common here — even though crime has been falling.
explanation: One close story outweighed the wider trend.
reflectionQuestion: Has one person's story ever changed how safe you feel?
whyHumansDoThis: Stories from people we know feel real and relevant — more than numbers on a page.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between a personally vivid story and an actual trend.

statement: A colleague was late to the meeting. Everyone assumed they're lazy — not knowing their car broke down.
explanation: Character got blamed before circumstances were considered.
reflectionQuestion: Next time someone's late — what situation might explain it?
whyHumansDoThis: We don't see others' full context, so we fill the gap with personality labels.
learningObjective: Learn to consider circumstances before judging character.

statement: Someone cut me off in traffic. I thought 'what a jerk' — without wondering if they had an emergency.
explanation: The action was blamed on character, not situation.
reflectionQuestion: Have you ever driven badly because of your own bad day?
whyHumansDoThis: It's faster to judge a stranger's character than imagine their unseen story.
learningObjective: Learn that the same behavior looks different depending on whether you know the situation behind it.

statement: A friend cancelled last minute again. I decided they're unreliable — maybe it was just a run of bad luck.
explanation: One label stuck before a real pattern was clear.
reflectionQuestion: How many times before you'd call something a pattern, not bad luck?
whyHumansDoThis: One clear story feels easier than holding uncertainty over time.
learningObjective: Learn to separate a single incident from an established pattern before judging someone's character.

statement: I passed because I'm smart — but failed last time because the questions were unfair.
explanation: Success went to me; failure went to outside factors.
reflectionQuestion: Your last win and loss — did you explain them the same way?
whyHumansDoThis: Crediting ourselves for wins and blaming outside forces for losses protects how we feel about ourselves.
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same standard of explanation to both your successes and failures.

statement: We won because of great strategy. We lost because of a bad referee.
explanation: The win was ours; the loss was someone else's fault.
reflectionQuestion: A loss you blamed entirely on something outside your control?
whyHumansDoThis: Keeping a positive self-image feels better than sitting with our own shortcomings.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate wins and losses with the same level of scrutiny.

statement: A manager took credit when a project worked — and blamed the team when a similar one failed.
explanation: Success was personal; failure was shifted away.
reflectionQuestion: When your project fails — how much do you own versus blame others?
whyHumansDoThis: Protecting our sense of competence is easier than sharing responsibility for failure.
learningObjective: Learn to look for your own contribution to both successes and failures, not just the convenient one.

statement: I keep holding a failing stock because I've already put so much into it — hoping it'll bounce back.
explanation: Past money spent is driving the choice, not future prospects.
reflectionQuestion: If you were deciding fresh today, would you still hold it?
whyHumansDoThis: Walking away feels like admitting a mistake. Staying avoids that pain — even when it costs more.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate decisions based on future outcomes, not past investment.

statement: I've spent five years in this relationship. I can't leave now — even though I'm unhappy.
explanation: Time already spent is treated as a reason to stay.
reflectionQuestion: Would you start this relationship today, knowing what you know?
whyHumansDoThis: Leaving can feel like wasted years were wasted for nothing. That's painful.
learningObjective: Learn that past time invested doesn't change what's the best choice going forward.

statement: We keep eating a mediocre meal we already paid for — even though we could stop.
explanation: Money already spent shouldn't change whether we keep eating.
reflectionQuestion: Does having paid change how much you 'should' finish?
whyHumansDoThis: We mix up 'already spent' with 'what to do next' — even when they're separate decisions.
learningObjective: Learn to separate a past, unrecoverable cost from the decision in front of you right now.

statement: Nine compliments and one criticism in my review — and I obsessed over the criticism all week.
explanation: One negative note outweighed nine positive ones.
reflectionQuestion: Last feedback you got — praise or criticism stuck more?
whyHumansDoThis: Bad news used to signal danger. Our brains still give it extra weight.
learningObjective: Learn to consciously weigh positive and negative feedback more evenly.

statement: News runs more bad stories than good ones — because negative headlines get more clicks.
explanation: Outlets and audiences both lean toward negative news.
reflectionQuestion: Do you click negative headlines more than positive ones?
whyHumansDoThis: Threats and bad news pull our attention harder than good news.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when media incentives are exploiting a psychological bias you already have.

statement: One critical comment from my partner stuck more than months of compliments.
explanation: A single negative remark dominated the emotional memory.
reflectionQuestion: One comment that overshadowed a lot of good moments?
whyHumansDoThis: Negative moments are processed more deeply and remembered more vividly.
learningObjective: Learn to actively track the ratio of positive to negative moments, rather than trusting your gut feeling.

statement: A polished, articulate candidate seemed competent at the technical work too — without any proof.
explanation: A good impression in one area spilled into another.
reflectionQuestion: Assumed someone was good at their job because they seemed confident?
whyHumansDoThis: One overall impression is easier than judging each skill separately.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate specific skills on their own evidence, not on general impressions.

statement: A celebrity endorsed a health product — so fans assumed it works, because they like the celebrity.
explanation: Feelings about the person transferred to the product.
reflectionQuestion: Would you trust the claim as much from a stranger?
whyHumansDoThis: We pass trust from people we like to things they're linked with.
learningObjective: Learn to separate your feelings about a person from the evidence for their claims.

statement: A charismatic colleague got promoted fast — and people assumed all their ideas were good, untested ones included.
explanation: Likeability stretched to ideas without evidence.
reflectionQuestion: How do you separate liking someone from trusting their untested ideas?
whyHumansDoThis: A strong first impression is hard to update — even when proof is missing.
learningObjective: Learn that likeability and idea quality are two separate things, even if they feel linked.

statement: A new hire was late on day one. Colleagues decided they're unreliable and probably not hardworking.
explanation: One slip became a whole personality judgment.
reflectionQuestion: One early mistake that colored how you saw someone?
whyHumansDoThis: One flaw gets simplified into 'this person is generally bad' instead of staying specific.
learningObjective: Learn to keep a single flaw separate from your overall judgment of a person's other qualities.

statement: Someone mispronounced a word in a talk — and the audience doubted whether they know the topic at all.
explanation: A small slip was stretched into a competence judgment.
reflectionQuestion: Judged someone's expertise over an unrelated small mistake?
whyHumansDoThis: Our brain wants one consistent story — and one flaw builds a negative one fast.
learningObjective: Learn to judge competence based on relevant evidence, not unrelated small errors.

statement: A public figure got one fact wrong — and viewers dismissed everything else they said, even the solid parts.
explanation: One error discredited the whole message.
reflectionQuestion: When someone errs once, do you dismiss everything they say?
whyHumansDoThis: One clear mistake feels like reliable evidence — even when it isn't about everything else.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate each claim on its own evidence rather than by association with an unrelated error.

statement: Success books interview winning entrepreneurs — not the many who tried the same thing and failed.
explanation: We only hear from survivors, so strategies look more reliable than they are.
reflectionQuestion: Reading a success story — do you ask who tried the same and failed?
whyHumansDoThis: Failures are quieter, less visible, and less fun to talk about.
learningObjective: Learn to actively look for the invisible failures behind visible success stories.

statement: 'These five habits made CEOs successful' — with no check on failed CEOs who had the same habits.
explanation: Without a comparison group, the link to success is shaky.
reflectionQuestion: Without asking who failed, can you trust why someone succeeded?
whyHumansDoThis: Success stories are celebrated. Quiet failures disappear from view.
learningObjective: Learn to ask for the comparison group before trusting a claim about what causes success.

statement: Everyone still in the course says it's easy — but the people who struggled already dropped out.
explanation: Only survivors are in the sample you're hearing from.
reflectionQuestion: Whose voices might be missing from the feedback you hear?
whyHumansDoThis: We hear from who's left — and forget who already walked away.
learningObjective: Learn to consider who is missing from a sample before drawing a conclusion.

statement: After a stock crashed, I said 'I knew that would happen' — though I'd bought more the week before.
explanation: Memory of what we believed shifted to match the outcome.
reflectionQuestion: Claimed you 'knew' something — despite acting the opposite before?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we know the ending, it feels inevitable — and our memory bends to match.
learningObjective: Learn to check your past predictions honestly instead of assuming you always saw it coming.

statement: After the loss, I insisted 'I knew we'd lose' — though I'd predicted a win the day before.
explanation: Recollection of the prediction was rewritten after the fact.
reflectionQuestion: Could you find what you actually predicted — versus what you claim now?
whyHumansDoThis: We rebuild past beliefs to fit what we know now.
learningObjective: Learn that memory of a prediction is not a reliable substitute for the prediction itself.

statement: After a project failed, the team said 'the risks were obvious from the start' — though nobody flagged them in planning.
explanation: Risks look obvious only after failure is known.
reflectionQuestion: Were the 'obvious' risks written down before the outcome?
whyHumansDoThis: Knowing how it ended makes the path feel more predictable than it was.
learningObjective: Learn to check documented predictions rather than trusting a reconstructed memory of what was 'obvious.'

statement: I assume most people share my political view — because everyone in my circle does.
explanation: My bubble was mistaken for the whole population.
reflectionQuestion: How would you check if most people agree — beyond your circle?
whyHumansDoThis: We're surrounded by similar people, so their views feel like everyone's.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between the views of your social circle and the views of the wider population.

statement: I posted a hot take online and got slammed — I'd assumed 'everyone thinks this.'
explanation: My bubble felt like consensus.
reflectionQuestion: Online backlash that surprised you because you thought your view was common?
whyHumansDoThis: Our own beliefs feel normal — so we assume others share them.
learningObjective: Learn that a view feeling normal to you doesn't mean it's widely shared.

statement: Two people agreed in a meeting — so the manager assumed the whole team supports the policy.
explanation: A vocal few stood in for everyone.
reflectionQuestion: When few speak up — what can you really conclude about the rest?
whyHumansDoThis: Loud agreement is easy to notice. Silent disagreement is invisible.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between a vocal minority and true group consensus.

statement: Same mistake from my department gets a pass — from another department, it's a serious problem.
explanation: Group membership changed how the mistake was judged.
reflectionQuestion: Judge mistakes from 'your people' more gently than from others?
whyHumansDoThis: We extend more trust and leniency to people we see as 'us.'
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same standard of judgment regardless of group membership.

statement: Our player's foul was accidental. Their player's identical foul was dirty play.
explanation: Same action, different story — depending on the team.
reflectionQuestion: Would you see it the same way if the teams were swapped?
whyHumansDoThis: Loyalty shapes how we read ambiguous actions — we favor our side.
learningObjective: Learn to test your judgment by imagining the same action from the 'other side.'

statement: I excuse a scandal from my party — but I'm outraged by the same thing from the other side.
explanation: Group allegiance changed the moral judgment.
reflectionQuestion: Would this bother you as much from your own side?
whyHumansDoThis: Protecting our group's reputation can feel like protecting part of ourselves.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when group loyalty, not the facts, is driving your judgment.

statement: Marketing sees lots of personalities in marketing — but assumes everyone in engineering is the same.
explanation: Diversity inside my group; sameness assumed outside it.
reflectionQuestion: Which groups do you see as diverse — and which do you lump together?
whyHumansDoThis: We know individuals in our group. Other groups we only see from a distance.
learningObjective: Learn that unfamiliarity, not actual similarity, often drives the perception that 'they're all the same.'

statement: I've never lived there — but I'm sure people from that country all think the same on this issue.
explanation: Limited exposure became a uniform stereotype.
reflectionQuestion: How much real exposure do you have to the group you're generalizing?
whyHumansDoThis: Less detail about a group means broader, simpler assumptions.
learningObjective: Learn to question generalizations about groups you don't know well.

statement: A headline says 'young people today' all think one way — but we'd never describe our own generation that simply.
explanation: A diverse group was flattened into one attitude.
reflectionQuestion: Would you accept this generalization about a group you're in?
whyHumansDoThis: Big categories are easier to write and think about than real diversity.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when a large group is being described as if it were one single, uniform entity.

statement: '90% survival rate' sounds great. '10% mortality rate' sounds scary. Same treatment.
explanation: Identical stats, different emotional frame.
reflectionQuestion: A decision where wording changed how you felt?
whyHumansDoThis: We react to gain vs loss framing before we sit with the numbers.
learningObjective: Learn to translate framed information into neutral terms before deciding.

statement: '90% fat free' sells better than '10% fat' — same product on the label.
explanation: Positive wording beat equivalent negative wording.
reflectionQuestion: Chosen a product because of the label, not the content?
whyHumansDoThis: Positive-sounding words trigger a warmer gut reaction — facts aside.
learningObjective: Learn to check the actual numbers behind marketing language, not just the wording used.

statement: One side says a policy saves 200 lives. The other says it fails to save 100 — same outcome, different frame.
explanation: Same numbers, opposite emotional pull.
reflectionQuestion: Do you flip the frame to see if the numbers still hold?
whyHumansDoThis: Communicators pick the frame that triggers the feeling they want.
learningObjective: Learn to reframe a statistic in the opposite direction before forming an opinion about it.

statement: Losing $1,000 hurts more than gaining $1,000 feels good — same amount.
explanation: Losses weigh heavier than equal gains.
reflectionQuestion: Would you take a 50/50 bet to win or lose $100?
whyHumansDoThis: Avoiding loss mattered more for survival than chasing equal rewards.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when the fear of losing is outweighing a rational evaluation of the actual stakes.

statement: In a negotiation, I'll fight harder to keep what I have than to gain something new of equal value.
explanation: Losing what we have feels worse than gaining the same amount.
reflectionQuestion: Last negotiation — fighting harder to keep or to gain?
whyHumansDoThis: Losses are felt more intensely than equivalent gains — often about twice as much.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate gains and losses of equal size with the same level of effort.

statement: I keep a gym membership I never use — canceling feels like losing something.
explanation: Avoiding the feeling of loss beats the logic of stopping payment.
reflectionQuestion: Keeping something unused — value, or just fear of losing it?
whyHumansDoThis: 'Losing' something — even unused — can outweigh the clear case for letting go.
learningObjective: Learn to separate the feeling of loss from the actual future costs and benefits of a decision.

statement: I smoke but tell myself I'm less likely to get sick than other smokers — no real reason why.
explanation: 'It won't happen to me' without evidence.
reflectionQuestion: A risk you've downplayed for yourself but not for others?
whyHumansDoThis: Believing we're the exception reduces anxiety and keeps us moving.
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same risk assessment to yourself that you would to someone else in your situation.

statement: We always estimate projects will finish early — despite a long history of running late.
explanation: Past delays get ignored for a hopeful new estimate.
reflectionQuestion: Does planning use real history — or just this project's best case?
whyHumansDoThis: We picture the best outcome for our own plans and discount past slip-ups.
learningObjective: Learn to use historical data, not just hopeful intuition, when estimating future outcomes.

statement: I'm not saving for retirement — I'll 'figure it out later' and assume I'll earn more than stats suggest.
explanation: A rosier-than-average future assumed without proof.
reflectionQuestion: Is your plan based on realistic odds — or a hoped-for best case?
whyHumansDoThis: An optimistic future feels easier than facing uncertain odds.
learningObjective: Learn to plan around realistic, evidence-based expectations rather than a hoped-for best case.

statement: I stayed in my company's default retirement plan — even though a better one needed just one form.
explanation: No action beat a slightly better option.
reflectionQuestion: A default choice you've never actually reconsidered?
whyHumansDoThis: Sticking with now takes no effort. Change takes a decision.
learningObjective: Learn to periodically re-evaluate default choices instead of assuming they're still the best option.

statement: We keep outdated software because switching feels risky — though the new system would clearly save time and money.
explanation: Familiar beat a rational case for change.
reflectionQuestion: Something at work you keep because 'that's how it's always been'?
whyHumansDoThis: Change means uncertainty. Staying put feels safe and predictable.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate the current approach against alternatives on their actual merits, not just familiarity.

statement: I've kept my phone's default settings for years — assuming the manufacturer picked the best ones.
explanation: Default was treated as best without checking.
reflectionQuestion: Default settings you've never questioned — phone, habits, anything?
whyHumansDoThis: Defaults feel like a recommendation. Changing them takes mental effort.
learningObjective: Learn to distinguish between 'default' and 'best,' since they aren't always the same thing.

statement: After one online course, I felt like an expert in a field professionals study for years.
explanation: A little learning created a lot of confidence.
reflectionQuestion: A skill where early confidence later met how much you didn't know?
whyHumansDoThis: Early learning skips the part where you discover how much is still ahead.
learningObjective: Learn that early confidence in a new skill often reflects unawareness of its full complexity, not real mastery.

statement: As a beginner, I'm sure I could beat a chess grandmaster with the right strategy.
explanation: Limited experience hid how wide the skill gap really is.
reflectionQuestion: How do you find out how good you really are at something new?
whyHumansDoThis: Without expert knowledge, it's hard to see what expert skill actually looks like.
learningObjective: Learn to seek outside, expert feedback to calibrate your own skill level accurately.

statement: A few articles online — and I felt more sure about my symptoms than the doctor with thousands of cases.
explanation: Surface reading felt like real expertise.
reflectionQuestion: When is your own research enough — and when do you need a pro?
whyHumansDoThis: A little information can feel complete when depth is still missing.
learningObjective: Learn to recognize the difference between surface-level familiarity and deep expertise.

statement: I trusted a diet tip from my doctor — even though it wasn't their area of expertise.
explanation: General authority stretched to a specific claim.
reflectionQuestion: Does their authority actually match this specific advice?
whyHumansDoThis: Titles and status become shortcuts for trust — instead of checking each claim.
learningObjective: Learn to check whether someone's authority is actually relevant to the specific claim they're making.

statement: I didn't question a flawed plan because a senior executive proposed it.
explanation: Rank stood in for a sound argument.
reflectionQuestion: Gone along with an idea mainly because of who suggested it?
whyHumansDoThis: Deferring to authority feels safer than scrutinizing the idea itself.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate ideas on their own merits, separate from the status of the person proposing them.

statement: An actor who played a doctor on TV comments on real medicine — and viewers partly trust them because of the role.
explanation: A fictional role lent unearned credibility.
reflectionQuestion: Would you trust this take as much if you didn't know who was speaking?
whyHumansDoThis: Familiarity with an authority-like role can feel like real expertise.
learningObjective: Learn to check whether a person's actual expertise, not their perceived role, supports their claim.

statement: I told myself the report would take an hour. Three hours later, I was still finishing tables.
explanation: The estimate was far too optimistic compared with what actually happened.
reflectionQuestion: What task recently took you much longer than you planned?
whyHumansDoThis: We picture the smooth version of a task and forget the delays, edits, and interruptions that usually show up.
learningObjective: Learn to compare new time estimates with how long similar tasks really took.

statement: We left for the airport 'with plenty of time' — and still sprinted to the gate because packing took longer than expected.
explanation: Travel time was planned as if everything would go perfectly.
reflectionQuestion: When travelling, do you build in buffer time or hope for the best?
whyHumansDoThis: A best-case mental movie feels realistic in advance, even when past trips told a different story.
learningObjective: Learn that travel and prep tasks usually need margin, not ideal-case timing.

statement: 'I'll just tidy the kitchen before guests arrive' — then the whole evening slipped because small jobs kept appearing.
explanation: A quick job was treated as quick without room for the usual extras.
reflectionQuestion: What 'small' job recently grew much bigger than you expected?
whyHumansDoThis: We estimate the main task and forget the side tasks that almost always come with it.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when a simple task is being underestimated because hidden steps are ignored.

statement: One bad week at work made me feel like I'm failing — even though the last six months went well.
explanation: The latest week weighed more than the longer track record.
reflectionQuestion: Has a recent rough patch ever overshadowed a longer stretch of good progress?
whyHumansDoThis: Fresh events are vivid and easy to recall, so they can feel like the whole story.
learningObjective: Learn to widen the time window before judging your overall performance.

statement: After one great month of saving, I felt rich — and spent more, forgetting the tighter months before.
explanation: The latest month shaped the feeling more than the fuller pattern.
reflectionQuestion: Do recent wins or losses change your spending more than your average month?
whyHumansDoThis: What just happened feels most real right now, even when the longer pattern matters more.
learningObjective: Learn to check your average pattern, not only your latest result.

statement: My friend cancelled once last week, so I thought they were pulling away — though they'd been reliable for years.
explanation: One recent event was treated as the new normal.
reflectionQuestion: When did a single recent moment change how you saw someone overall?
whyHumansDoThis: The latest interaction is bright in memory and can drown out a longer history.
learningObjective: Learn to balance a recent event against the longer relationship pattern.

statement: Everyone at work switched to a new app, so I did too — without checking if it actually helped me.
explanation: Popularity became the reason to join in.
reflectionQuestion: Have you adopted something mainly because others already had?
whyHumansDoThis: If many people are doing it, it can feel validated — even before we test it ourselves.
learningObjective: Learn to separate 'everyone is doing it' from 'this is right for me.'

statement: I bought the coat everyone was posting online — and only later wondered if I actually liked it.
explanation: Trendiness pushed the choice more than personal preference.
reflectionQuestion: Anything in your home that felt like a trend purchase afterward?
whyHumansDoThis: Seeing many people choose something makes it feel safer and more correct.
learningObjective: Learn to pause when popularity is doing most of the convincing.

statement: The whole group wanted the same restaurant, so I went along — even though I wanted something quieter.
explanation: The group direction pulled the choice more than personal preference.
reflectionQuestion: When did you follow the crowd even though you wanted something different?
whyHumansDoThis: Going with the group reduces friction and can feel like the obvious move.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when group momentum is replacing your own preference.

statement: A song I initially skipped started feeling good after the café played it every day.
explanation: Repeated exposure created liking without any new reason.
reflectionQuestion: Something you disliked at first but warmed to just from hearing or seeing it often?
whyHumansDoThis: Familiarity feels comfortable, and comfort can be mistaken for quality.
learningObjective: Learn that liking can grow from repetition alone.

statement: The new colleague seemed awkward at first — then likeable after a few lunches together.
explanation: Familiar contact warmed the impression over time.
reflectionQuestion: Someone who grew on you mainly because you saw them more often?
whyHumansDoThis: Repeated contact lowers uncertainty and makes people feel easier to be around.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when familiarity is improving your opinion more than new information is.

statement: I chose the logo design we'd seen in every mock-up — not because it was best, but because it felt right.
explanation: Repeated viewing made one option feel more natural.
reflectionQuestion: Have you preferred the version you saw most often, not the one you judged most carefully?
whyHumansDoThis: What we see repeatedly feels more true, safe, and 'ours.'
learningObjective: Learn to separate repeated exposure from genuine preference.

statement: I wouldn't pay £80 for that mug in a shop — but I also don't want to sell mine for £80.
explanation: Owning it made the same mug feel worth more.
reflectionQuestion: Something you would not rebuy today but still do not want to let go?
whyHumansDoThis: Ownership attaches identity and memory, which inflates how valuable something feels.
learningObjective: Learn that ownership alone can change what price feels fair.

statement: Selling my old bike felt painful, even though I had not ridden it in two years.
explanation: Letting go of something owned felt costly beyond its use.
reflectionQuestion: Something you kept mainly because it was yours, not because you used it?
whyHumansDoThis: Giving up what we own can feel like losing part of ourselves, not just an object.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what an item is worth to you now, not what ownership added emotionally.

statement: I priced my childhood books higher than identical copies online — because they had been on my shelf.
explanation: Personal ownership pushed the price above the market norm.
reflectionQuestion: Have you overpriced something you were selling because it felt special to you?
whyHumansDoThis: Our things carry stories, and those stories can feel like extra market value.
learningObjective: Learn to separate sentimental value from what a buyer would actually pay.

statement: The coin landed heads four times, so tails felt 'due' on the next flip.
explanation: Past flips do not change the odds of the next independent flip.
reflectionQuestion: Have you ever expected luck to 'balance out' right away?
whyHumansDoThis: Random sequences can look like they owe us a correction, even when each event is fresh.
learningObjective: Learn that independent random events do not repay earlier outcomes.

statement: After three rainy weekends, I packed for sun — because it 'had to' change soon.
explanation: Weather was treated as if it should balance a streak, not start fresh each week.
reflectionQuestion: When have you expected a streak to end just because it had gone on long enough?
whyHumansDoThis: Streaks feel meaningful, but many natural patterns do not keep score for us.
learningObjective: Learn to ask whether a streak actually changes the next odds.

statement: Our team lost twice, so I felt the next match was almost guaranteed — as if losses had to be repaid.
explanation: Past results were treated as if they forced a coming correction.
reflectionQuestion: Have past results ever made you feel sure the next one would swing the other way?
whyHumansDoThis: We like stories where things even out, even when the next try is its own separate chance.
learningObjective: Learn to treat the next attempt on its own merits, not as debt repayment from earlier results.

statement: Three colleagues quit in one month, so I decided the company must be in trouble.
explanation: A short cluster was read as a meaningful signal from very little data.
reflectionQuestion: A small streak that made you sure something bigger was going on?
whyHumansDoThis: Our brains are pattern hunters, and a few similar events can look like proof.
learningObjective: Learn to ask how much data you really have before calling something a trend.

statement: Two buses arrived together, so I concluded the schedule was broken — though it might have been chance.
explanation: A clump in timing was treated as a system failure without enough evidence.
reflectionQuestion: When have a few coincidences felt like a clear pattern to you?
whyHumansDoThis: Clusters stand out in memory, while ordinary spacing is easy to forget.
learningObjective: Learn that clumps can happen by chance and still feel intentional.

statement: Three friends got colds the same week, so I blamed the restaurant — with no real link.
explanation: A short cluster was turned into a cause without checking how often that happens randomly.
reflectionQuestion: A coincidence you turned into a story with a culprit?
whyHumansDoThis: When events bunch together, we often invent a tidy explanation instead of sitting with randomness.
learningObjective: Learn to test whether a cluster is large enough to mean anything.

statement: Quiet, careful, loves reading — I guessed librarian, not salesperson, without checking the odds.
explanation: The description fit a stereotype more than it fit real-world frequency.
reflectionQuestion: When have you guessed someone's job or role from vibes alone?
whyHumansDoThis: A neat type-match feels convincing, even when the stereotype is rare in reality.
learningObjective: Learn that a good fit to a stereotype is not the same as a good probability estimate.

statement: The polished speaker seemed like a great analyst — though their numbers did not hold up.
explanation: They looked like the type of person who should be good at analysis.
reflectionQuestion: When has someone 'seemed like' the right person for a role before you checked the work?
whyHumansDoThis: We use prototypes to judge skill, because prototypes are easier than evidence.
learningObjective: Learn to check performance instead of type-fit when judging competence.

statement: A startup with a sleek office felt destined to succeed — more than a scrappy one with better sales.
explanation: Success was inferred from how the company looked, not how it performed.
reflectionQuestion: What surface cues have ever made something feel more successful than it was?
whyHumansDoThis: We expect success to look a certain way, so the look itself can sell the story.
learningObjective: Learn to separate appearance of success from actual results.

statement: After one dramatic news story, I worried the problem was everywhere — though it is actually rare.
explanation: A vivid case overpowered how uncommon the issue really is.
reflectionQuestion: A rare problem that felt common because one example was memorable?
whyHumansDoThis: Specific stories hit harder than background statistics, so the story can feel like the norm.
learningObjective: Learn to start with how common something is before judging from one example.

statement: The test came back positive, and I panicked — forgetting how often false positives happen in screening.
explanation: The result felt definitive without weighing how common false positives are.
reflectionQuestion: When have you reacted to one result without the broader odds?
whyHumansDoThis: A single alarming number feels personal and urgent, while base rates feel abstract.
learningObjective: Learn to combine test results with how often they are wrong in your group.

statement: She matched the profile perfectly, so I was sure she was the right hire — though most strong profiles still fail.
explanation: A good fit to the ideal profile ignored how few such candidates work out overall.
reflectionQuestion: When have you felt certain from a great match without the bigger success rate?
whyHumansDoThis: A perfect-seeming case can make us forget how often similar cases do not pan out.
learningObjective: Learn to ask how often similar-looking cases succeed, not only how good this one looks.

statement: The holiday was mostly fine, but one awful final day made me call the whole trip bad.
explanation: The ending reshaped memory more than the full week.
reflectionQuestion: A trip or event remembered mainly by its worst moment or ending?
whyHumansDoThis: Peaks and endings are vivid, so they can rewrite how the whole experience feels.
learningObjective: Learn that memory often weights the peak and ending more than the middle.

statement: The dentist visit hurt most at the end, so I remembered it as painful — though most of it was fine.
explanation: The final discomfort dominated the memory of the whole appointment.
reflectionQuestion: An experience where the ending coloured the whole memory?
whyHumansDoThis: How something ends leaves a strong stamp on what we take away.
learningObjective: Learn to recall the full timeline, not only the loudest or last moment.

statement: One amazing dessert at the end made me rave about a restaurant — even though the main course was average.
explanation: A strong ending lifted the whole meal in memory.
reflectionQuestion: Has a great ending ever made you recommend something more than the full experience deserved?
whyHumansDoThis: A high point at the close can make the whole episode feel better than it was minute by minute.
learningObjective: Learn to judge experiences across the whole arc, not only the highlight or finish.

statement: After buying the car, I kept noticing perks — and forgot the flaws I worried about in the showroom.
explanation: Memory tilted toward making the purchase look smarter after it was made.
reflectionQuestion: After a big purchase, did you start defending it more than you questioned it beforehand?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we choose, we want to feel we chose well — so the mind edits the story.
learningObjective: Learn that post-choice memory often flatters the option you picked.

statement: I moved cities and told everyone it was perfect — though I had serious doubts the week before.
explanation: The decision was remembered more positively after committing.
reflectionQuestion: A choice you talked up more after making it than while deciding?
whyHumansDoThis: Public commitment and private doubt do not sit well together, so recall gets rosier.
learningObjective: Learn to compare your after-the-fact story with what you felt before deciding.

statement: The holiday I picked now feels like the best option — even though the other plans looked just as good.
explanation: The chosen trip is remembered as clearly better than the alternatives.
reflectionQuestion: After choosing between good options, did the one you picked suddenly seem obviously best?
whyHumansDoThis: Choosing creates ownership, and ownership makes our option feel more justified.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when commitment is upgrading your memory of the chosen option.

statement: I pressed refresh three times, convinced it would make the page load faster.
explanation: A small action was treated as steering an outcome it could not affect.
reflectionQuestion: Any small ritual you do that feels like it helps, though it probably does not?
whyHumansDoThis: Doing something feels better than waiting helplessly, so action can feel like control.
learningObjective: Learn to spot when your actions cannot actually change the outcome.

statement: I picked my own lottery numbers, so they felt more likely to win than a random ticket.
explanation: Personal choice created a false sense of influence over random odds.
reflectionQuestion: Have you ever felt more hopeful about a chance because you chose the details yourself?
whyHumansDoThis: Choosing numbers ourselves makes the outcome feel connected to us, even when it is not.
learningObjective: Learn that personal involvement does not change random odds.

statement: When my team is losing, I change seats — as if where I sit could affect the score.
explanation: A personal action was linked to a result it cannot control.
reflectionQuestion: Any lucky routine you keep during games or stressful moments?
whyHumansDoThis: In uncertain moments, we reach for anything that might give us a handle on the result.
learningObjective: Learn to notice comfort rituals that feel like control but are not.

statement: I spilled coffee on my shirt and felt everyone stared — but nobody mentioned it all day.
explanation: The mistake felt highly visible, though others barely noticed.
reflectionQuestion: A small embarrassment that felt huge to you but probably passed unnoticed?
whyHumansDoThis: We live inside our own attention, so our flaws feel giant while others stay focused on themselves.
learningObjective: Learn that others usually notice your slip-ups far less than you do.

statement: I thought my bad haircut was obvious to everyone — my friends said they hadn't noticed.
explanation: A personal worry felt public, though it was mostly private.
reflectionQuestion: Something about your appearance that felt glaring, but others barely saw?
whyHumansDoThis: What bothers us about ourselves takes centre stage in our mind, not in everyone else's.
learningObjective: Learn to test your visibility fears against what others actually report noticing.

statement: I stumbled over one word in a talk and replayed it for days — the audience moved on in seconds.
explanation: One slip felt unforgettable to the speaker, but not to listeners.
reflectionQuestion: A minor mistake you magnified more than anyone else did?
whyHumansDoThis: We are each the star of our own show, so our blunders get a close-up in memory.
learningObjective: Learn that audiences usually weight your whole message, not one wobble.

statement: I said I'd cut back on sugar — then told myself one biscuit 'doesn't count' after a hard day.
explanation: The action clashed with the goal, so the rule got quietly rewritten.
reflectionQuestion: A promise to yourself that you explained away when you broke it?
whyHumansDoThis: When behaviour and beliefs collide, it is often easier to adjust the story than change the habit.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when you are reframing a rule to match what you already did.

statement: I spent a lot on a course I rarely use — so I started saying it is 'investing in myself.'
explanation: A costly choice was reframed to reduce the discomfort of not using it.
reflectionQuestion: Something you defended with a new story after realising you were not using it?
whyHumansDoThis: We dislike feeling wasteful or inconsistent, so we reshape the meaning of the choice.
learningObjective: Learn to spot when a new justification is easing discomfort, not adding value.

statement: I know smoking is bad for me, so I focus on the one uncle who smoked and lived to ninety.
explanation: A comforting exception was used to soften the clash between habit and health knowledge.
reflectionQuestion: An exception you hold onto when a habit bumps into what you know?
whyHumansDoThis: One counterexample can feel like permission when the main belief is uncomfortable to face.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when you are hunting exceptions to reduce mental discomfort.

statement: Even after the study was debunked, I still believed the original claim.
explanation: The belief survived after its main support was removed.
reflectionQuestion: Something you kept believing after the main reason for it fell apart?
whyHumansDoThis: Letting go of a settled belief takes effort, so the mind keeps patching it instead.
learningObjective: Learn to ask whether you still believe something because the evidence supports it today.

statement: My colleague proved the process was fine, but I still felt sure they were the problem.
explanation: The original blame stuck even after the explanation changed.
reflectionQuestion: When has new information failed to shift a view you already held?
whyHumansDoThis: First impressions and early stories can keep running even when the facts move on.
learningObjective: Learn to update judgments when the supporting reasons have been overturned.

statement: The restaurant got great reviews later, but I still called it bad because of one visit years ago.
explanation: An old judgment held on after newer evidence improved.
reflectionQuestion: A place or person you judged early and never really reconsidered?
whyHumansDoThis: Early beliefs become familiar, and familiar beliefs feel true even when life updates.
learningObjective: Learn to reopen old judgments when meaningful new evidence appears.

statement: Storm warnings came in, but I stayed home because bad weather 'never really hits us here.'
explanation: Past routine made disruption feel unlikely, despite the warning.
reflectionQuestion: A warning you downplayed because trouble had not happened locally before?
whyHumansDoThis: What has always been normal feels like what will keep being normal, until it does not.
learningObjective: Learn that a calm history does not guarantee a calm future.

statement: The smoke alarm beeped oddly, but I kept cooking because fires 'don't happen to careful people.'
explanation: Ordinary life felt too normal for a serious risk to feel real.
reflectionQuestion: A safety sign or signal you brushed off because the day felt ordinary?
whyHumansDoThis: Daily routine is comforting, and comfort can make rare dangers feel unreal.
learningObjective: Learn to treat warnings seriously even when your routine has been smooth.

statement: People said the company was struggling, but I ignored it — we'd been stable for years.
explanation: Long stability made warning signs feel less believable.
reflectionQuestion: When has 'it's always been fine' made you slow to react?
whyHumansDoThis: A long stretch of normal makes change feel hypothetical, even when signs are showing.
learningObjective: Learn to weigh warning signs even when your own experience has been steady.

statement: I explained the form in two minutes — then watched a new starter get lost on step one.
explanation: What felt obvious to me skipped steps that were not obvious to a beginner.
reflectionQuestion: Something that feels simple to you now but was confusing when you started?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we know a thing, it is hard to remember what it was like not to know it.
learningObjective: Learn to explain from the listener's starting point, not your current one.

statement: My parent kept asking basic phone questions, and I got impatient — forgetting I once needed the same help.
explanation: My skill made their struggle feel harder to understand than it should have.
reflectionQuestion: When have you forgotten how recently something was new to you too?
whyHumansDoThis: Competence can make other people's learning pace feel surprisingly slow.
learningObjective: Learn to remember your own beginner stage when teaching someone else.

statement: The teacher said the maths was 'obvious' — but half the class was still lost on the first step.
explanation: Expert ease made the gap invisible to the person explaining.
reflectionQuestion: A teacher or guide who skipped steps that were not obvious to you?
whyHumansDoThis: Experts often see the whole path at once, while learners still need the first stones pointed out.
learningObjective: Learn to test explanations on someone who does not already know the answer.

statement: Every time I wear this shirt, meetings go well — so now I call it my lucky shirt.
explanation: Two things were linked in memory without evidence they actually connect.
reflectionQuestion: A lucky item or routine you linked to success without checking the misses?
whyHumansDoThis: Memorable pairings stick, while the times they did not happen together fade away.
learningObjective: Learn to check how often the effect happens without the supposed cause.

statement: I ate sugar and got a headache — so I blamed sugar, ignoring the many sweet days with no headache.
explanation: One vivid pairing created a cause link stronger than the data supports.
reflectionQuestion: A food or habit you blamed because one memorable day lined up?
whyHumansDoThis: When two things happen together once and stand out, our brain files them as partners.
learningObjective: Learn to count the non-events before declaring a personal cause.

statement: Two rude people had the same accent, so I started expecting rudeness from everyone with it.
explanation: A small memorable pair turned into a broader link without enough evidence.
reflectionQuestion: A trait you linked to a group after only a couple of examples?
whyHumansDoThis: Striking examples are easy to remember and can feel like a rule after very little data.
learningObjective: Learn to question links built from a few vivid cases.

statement: A quiet neighbour who reads poetry feels more likely to be a librarian and a volunteer than just a librarian.
explanation: A richer story felt more likely, even though adding details always lowers the odds.
reflectionQuestion: A detailed description that felt more believable because it was more specific?
whyHumansDoThis: Specific stories match our imagination better, so they can feel more probable than simpler truths.
learningObjective: Learn that extra detail can make a story feel more likely while actually making it less likely.

statement: The email scam with a full backstory felt more real than a plain 'your account is locked' message.
explanation: Added detail made the scam feel more plausible than a simpler version would.
reflectionQuestion: When has a detailed story felt more credible just because it was detailed?
whyHumansDoThis: Detail creates a vivid picture, and vivid pictures feel like truth.
learningObjective: Learn to distrust plausibility that comes from story detail rather than evidence.

statement: I thought 'late because of traffic and a flat tyre' was more likely than simply 'late because of traffic.'
explanation: The more specific excuse felt more believable, though it is statistically less likely.
reflectionQuestion: A detailed explanation that sounded more convincing because it had more parts?
whyHumansDoThis: More pieces can make a story hang together in our minds, even when simpler versions are more common.
learningObjective: Learn that simpler explanations are often more probable than dramatic detailed ones.

statement: I'd heard the health claim so many times that it started sounding true — even though I never checked it.
explanation: Repetition created a feeling of truth without verification.
reflectionQuestion: A claim that felt true mostly because you had heard it often?
whyHumansDoThis: Familiar phrases are easier to process, and easy can be mistaken for correct.
learningObjective: Learn that repeated claims need checking, not just familiarity.

statement: The same rumour kept appearing in my feed, so I began to believe it — no source ever checked out.
explanation: Seeing it again and again made it feel credible.
reflectionQuestion: Something online that felt true because you kept seeing it, not because it was verified?
whyHumansDoThis: Repetition builds familiarity, and familiarity quietly borrows the feeling of accuracy.
learningObjective: Learn to pause when repetition is doing the persuading.

statement: A catchy slogan from the ad campaign stuck in my head — and later felt like a fact.
explanation: Hearing the line often made it feel factual, not just memorable.
reflectionQuestion: An advertising line or slogan that started to feel like common knowledge?
whyHumansDoThis: What we hear repeatedly becomes mentally smooth, and smooth can feel true.
learningObjective: Learn to separate memorable wording from verified information.

statement: When they're late, I think they're rude. When I'm late, it's because the morning was impossible.
explanation: The same lateness gets a character story for them and a situation story for me.
reflectionQuestion: A time you blamed someone's character but blamed circumstances for yourself?
whyHumansDoThis: We know our own context from the inside, but only see others from the outside.
learningObjective: Learn to apply the same kind of explanation to others that you use for yourself.

statement: My colleague snapped and I called them difficult — but when I snapped, it was stress.
explanation: Their behaviour became a trait; mine became a one-off caused by pressure.
reflectionQuestion: When have you labelled someone else's mood as 'who they are' but yours as 'what happened'?
whyHumansDoThis: Our own reasons feel vivid and situational, while other people's actions look like personality.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what pressure might explain someone else's behaviour before naming their character.

statement: He failed the test because he's lazy, I failed because the questions were unfair.
explanation: Failure was explained by character for him and by circumstances for me.
reflectionQuestion: A failure you explained with context, but judged someone else's with personality?
whyHumansDoThis: Protecting our self-image and knowing our own story makes double standards easy to miss.
learningObjective: Learn to compare how you explain your outcomes with how you explain others' outcomes.

statement: My friends are so confirmation-biased — I, on the other hand, just look at facts.
explanation: Bias was easy to see in others and invisible in myself.
reflectionQuestion: A bias you notice quickly in others but rarely in yourself?
whyHumansDoThis: Biases feel like clear distortions in other people and like common sense in us.
learningObjective: Learn that spotting bias in others does not mean you are free of it.

statement: Everyone at the meeting was being political — except me, I was just being honest.
explanation: Motives were assigned to others while my own view felt neutral.
reflectionQuestion: When have you felt like the one objective person in a heated room?
whyHumansDoThis: Our own motives feel clear and justified from the inside, so they do not look like bias.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what your own interests might be colouring, not only other people's.

statement: I can list five biases my partner has — but struggle to name one I might share.
explanation: Bias detection worked better outward than inward.
reflectionQuestion: Could you name a bias you have shown this month, not just one you've noticed in others?
whyHumansDoThis: Bias is easier to spot at a distance, especially when it is not threatening our self-image.
learningObjective: Learn to turn bias-spotting inward with the same energy you use on others.

statement: I don't get how anyone could watch that and not see the obvious truth.
explanation: My view felt like plain reality, not one perspective among many.
reflectionQuestion: A disagreement where you felt the other side must be ignoring obvious facts?
whyHumansDoThis: Our own experience feels direct, so different views can seem irrational instead of different.
learningObjective: Learn that your view can feel like reality itself while still being a perspective.

statement: If people were thinking clearly, they'd agree with me on this — disagreement means they're biased.
explanation: Agreement was treated as the mark of clear thinking.
reflectionQuestion: When have you assumed disagreement meant the other person was not reasoning properly?
whyHumansDoThis: We experience our conclusions as clear sight, so conflict can feel like someone else's distortion.
learningObjective: Learn to treat disagreement as a clue about different information or values, not only bad thinking.

statement: The facts are right in front of us — I don't see how there are two sides to this.
explanation: One interpretation was mistaken for the only reasonable reading of the facts.
reflectionQuestion: An issue where you felt the answer was plain, but others sincerely saw it differently?
whyHumansDoThis: When evidence fits our view neatly, it is easy to forget that others may weigh it differently.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what the other side is seeing, not only why they are 'wrong.'

statement: After the theft, people asked what he did to make himself a target — instead of blaming the thief.
explanation: The victim was searched for a reason the bad outcome 'made sense.'
reflectionQuestion: A bad event where people looked for what the person 'did wrong' to deserve it?
whyHumansDoThis: A fair-world story is comforting, so we sometimes rewrite victims into causes.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when you are searching for desert instead of looking at harm.

statement: She got laid off, and I caught myself thinking she must not have worked hard enough.
explanation: A painful outcome was explained as something earned rather than unlucky.
reflectionQuestion: When have you quietly assumed someone's misfortune reflected their effort or character?
whyHumansDoThis: Believing the world is fair can push us to blame people for outcomes luck also shapes.
learningObjective: Learn to separate outcomes from moral judgments about who deserved them.

statement: The successful neighbour 'must have earned it' — the struggling one 'must have made bad choices.'
explanation: Success and struggle were both turned into moral stories.
reflectionQuestion: Do you explain other people's luck with effort stories more than your own?
whyHumansDoThis: A tidy moral universe feels safer than one where chance plays a big role.
learningObjective: Learn to leave room for luck and context when judging other people's outcomes.

statement: Their short email felt deliberately cold — though they might just have been busy.
explanation: Ambiguous tone was read as intentional hostility.
reflectionQuestion: A brief message you interpreted as rude before checking what was going on?
whyHumansDoThis: When intent is unclear, threat detection can fill the gap with a negative story.
learningObjective: Learn to ask for neutral explanations before assuming someone meant harm.

statement: A friend didn't reply for a day, so I decided they were annoyed with me.
explanation: Silence was treated as intentional negative intent.
reflectionQuestion: When have you read hurt feelings into a delay that had nothing to do with you?
whyHumansDoThis: Social uncertainty is uncomfortable, and a clear negative reason can feel easier than not knowing.
learningObjective: Learn to treat silence or ambiguity as unclear, not automatically hostile.

statement: Someone bumped me in the shop and I assumed they did it on purpose — they were just distracted.
explanation: A small accident was read as deliberate aggression.
reflectionQuestion: A minor bump or glance you took as intentional when it probably was not?
whyHumansDoThis: Under uncertainty, our threat radar can upgrade accidents into insults.
learningObjective: Learn to default to accident or distraction before assuming hostile intent.

statement: Looking back, I remember doing most of the group project — my teammates remember differently.
explanation: My own contribution loomed larger in memory than it did for others.
reflectionQuestion: A shared task where you remember carrying more than others say you did?
whyHumansDoThis: We live through our own effort in detail, so it naturally feels bigger in recall.
learningObjective: Learn to compare your memory of effort with other people's accounts.

statement: I tell the story like I saved the day — though three people handled the crisis together.
explanation: My role grew in the retelling compared with the shared reality.
reflectionQuestion: A group win you later described as mostly your doing?
whyHumansDoThis: Our own actions are vivid and central to us, so memory can quietly promote us to lead role.
learningObjective: Learn to check whether your story gives others the share they actually had.

statement: Years later I still remember my line in the school play more than anyone else's.
explanation: Personal involvement made my part the main memory.
reflectionQuestion: A shared event you mostly remember through your own part in it?
whyHumansDoThis: What we personally did or said is stored more richly than what others did around us.
learningObjective: Learn that shared memories are often self-centred even when they feel objective.

statement: I was nervous giving the toast and felt everyone could tell — they said they had no idea.
explanation: Inner anxiety felt obvious outside, but it was mostly hidden.
reflectionQuestion: A feeling you were sure showed on your face, but nobody noticed?
whyHumansDoThis: We feel our emotions strongly from the inside, so we overestimate how visible they are outside.
learningObjective: Learn that inner feelings are usually less readable than they seem.

statement: I thought my disappointment was obvious in the meeting, so I didn't say anything — and my manager was surprised when I brought it up later.
explanation: I assumed my mood was showing clearly when it was not.
reflectionQuestion: A time you stayed quiet because you thought people already knew how you felt?
whyHumansDoThis: Because we feel our state so clearly, we expect others can read it without words.
learningObjective: Learn to say important feelings out loud instead of assuming they are visible.

statement: During the argument I was sure my hurt was written all over me — my partner said I looked calm.
explanation: Emotional intensity inside did not match how I appeared outside.
reflectionQuestion: An emotion that felt unmistakable to you but invisible to someone else?
whyHumansDoThis: Inner experience is loud to us and often quiet to observers.
learningObjective: Learn not to rely on appearance alone as proof others understand your state.

statement: I'd rather have £40 today than £60 in a month — even though I know waiting is better.
explanation: The immediate amount pulled harder than the larger later one.
reflectionQuestion: A time you chose the sooner smaller reward over the better later one?
whyHumansDoThis: Right now feels real and urgent, while later gains feel abstract and easy to discount.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when immediacy is overweighting a better delayed option.

statement: I planned to save this month's bonus, but once it arrived I spent half on things I didn't need.
explanation: Having money now felt more compelling than the future plan.
reflectionQuestion: Money you intended to save but spent once it was actually in your account?
whyHumansDoThis: Future goals are easy in theory; present temptation is concrete and immediate.
learningObjective: Learn to protect future-you with rules made before the money arrives.

statement: I'll start the diet Monday — but when Monday comes, today's biscuit still wins.
explanation: Future discipline felt easy to promise, but present reward won.
reflectionQuestion: A plan you made for later that collapsed when later became now?
whyHumansDoThis: Tomorrow's willpower feels strong from today, but today has the biscuit in front of us.
learningObjective: Learn that intentions about the future often underestimate present temptation.

statement: I picked the plan with clear costs, even though the flexible one might have saved money.
explanation: Known terms felt safer than uncertain possible savings.
reflectionQuestion: A time you chose the clearer option because the unknown felt too risky?
whyHumansDoThis: Not knowing the odds can feel worse than a middling but known outcome.
learningObjective: Learn to compare unknown options fairly instead of avoiding them by default.

statement: The job with a clear role description appealed more than one with vague 'lots of opportunity.'
explanation: Clarity felt safer than an undefined upside.
reflectionQuestion: Have you preferred a clear path over a vague one, even when the vague one might be better?
whyHumansDoThis: Ambiguity makes outcomes hard to picture, so clear options get an unfair comfort bonus.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what the unclear option might be worth before rejecting it for vagueness alone.

statement: I skipped the new treatment because the side effects weren't fully known — and stuck with the older one I understood.
explanation: Unknown risks pushed the choice toward the familiar option.
reflectionQuestion: A decision where missing information made you stick with the known choice?
whyHumansDoThis: What we cannot measure feels like a bigger threat than risks we have learned to live with.
learningObjective: Learn to weigh unknowns carefully without treating all ambiguity as worse than known harm.

statement: One child's story on the news moved me to donate — statistics about thousands hadn't.
explanation: One named story stirred action more than large numbers did.
reflectionQuestion: A single story that moved you more than a bigger statistic about the same issue?
whyHumansDoThis: A face and a name make suffering feel real; numbers can stay abstract.
learningObjective: Learn that one vivid story can outweigh larger but less personal evidence.

statement: The office raised money fast for a colleague's sick parent — broader charity drives rarely do as well.
explanation: A known person in need sparked more giving than distant causes.
reflectionQuestion: When has someone you knew personally received more help than a larger anonymous need?
whyHumansDoThis: We respond strongly to people we can picture, especially when they feel close to us.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when closeness and identity are driving generosity more than scale of need.

statement: A campaign with one patient's photo got my attention — the chart with mortality rates did not.
explanation: One visible person carried the message more than the broader data.
reflectionQuestion: Health or charity appeals that worked on you because of one person, not the numbers?
whyHumansDoThis: Statistics describe many lives; one story feels like a life we can imagine standing in front of us.
learningObjective: Learn to let statistics matter even when one story feels more urgent.

statement: I bought one biscuit for later and said I'd stop at one — I ate four once I opened the pack.
explanation: Future self-control was overestimated before temptation arrived.
reflectionQuestion: A 'just one' promise that collapsed once the thing was in front of you?
whyHumansDoThis: In advance, resisting feels easy because the craving is not here yet.
learningObjective: Learn to plan for the moment of temptation, not the moment of planning.

statement: I told myself I'd only check my phone once during dinner — and picked it up every few minutes.
explanation: I predicted stronger restraint than I actually had in the moment.
reflectionQuestion: A limit you set for yourself that you broke almost immediately?
whyHumansDoThis: We picture our disciplined self without the pull of the actual trigger in front of us.
learningObjective: Learn to make temptation harder to reach instead of trusting willpower alone.

statement: Before the party I said I wouldn't drink much — by midnight I'd lost count.
explanation: Self-control looked stronger on the way in than it was in the room.
reflectionQuestion: Social plans where you were more confident beforehand than you turned out to be?
whyHumansDoThis: Social mood and cues are hard to simulate in advance, so restraint gets overbooked.
learningObjective: Learn to set guardrails before environments that weaken your restraint.

statement: The moment my parent told me to tidy up, I wanted to do it less — even though I had planned to.
explanation: Being told what to do sparked resistance instead of cooperation.
reflectionQuestion: Something you resisted mainly because someone ordered you to do it?
whyHumansDoThis: When freedom feels threatened, pushing back can feel more urgent than the task itself.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when resistance is about autonomy, not the actual request.

statement: A 'limited time only' message made me want the deal more — mostly because I hated being pushed.
explanation: Pressure to act quickly triggered pushback disguised as desire.
reflectionQuestion: A sales tactic that made you want to say no on principle?
whyHumansDoThis: Hard sells can feel like someone is grabbing the steering wheel, and we grab back.
learningObjective: Learn to separate whether you want something from whether you resent being nudged.

statement: My manager said I had to use the new form, so I dragged my feet — even though it might help.
explanation: Mandatory change sparked resistance more than the form itself did.
reflectionQuestion: A reasonable change you resisted because it was imposed, not proposed?
whyHumansDoThis: Choice matters to us, so commands can make decent ideas feel like battles.
learningObjective: Learn to judge the change on its merits, not only on how much freedom you felt you lost.

statement: I haven't opened my credit card app in weeks — the balance makes me tense.
explanation: Avoiding the information felt easier than facing the problem.
reflectionQuestion: A bill, email, or number you have been putting off looking at?
whyHumansDoThis: Not looking can briefly protect us from bad feelings, even when it leaves the issue untouched.
learningObjective: Learn that avoiding information rarely makes the underlying problem smaller.

statement: My knee has been sore for months, but I keep postponing the doctor because I don't want bad news.
explanation: Fear of confirmation led to delaying the check entirely.
reflectionQuestion: A health worry you have avoided investigating because you hoped it would go away?
whyHumansDoThis: Possible bad news can feel safer as a vague worry than as a confirmed fact.
learningObjective: Learn to face uncomfortable information early, when you still have more options.

statement: I saw the project was slipping but avoided the status meeting — hoping it would sort itself out.
explanation: I stepped away from information that would force a hard conversation.
reflectionQuestion: A problem at work you dodged because looking closely would make it real?
whyHumansDoThis: Meetings and updates can turn worry into action, and action can feel heavier than hope.
learningObjective: Learn to seek the update you are avoiding when you already suspect trouble.

statement: When my friend got promoted, part of me felt like that meant less chance for me.
explanation: Their gain was treated as my loss, even though both could succeed.
reflectionQuestion: Someone else's win that felt like it shrunk your opportunity?
whyHumansDoThis: Success can feel like a fixed pie, especially when we are competing for attention or roles.
learningObjective: Learn to ask whether another person's gain actually reduces yours.

statement: If the other team gets more budget, I assume ours has to lose out — even in a growing company.
explanation: Resources were treated as strictly either-or.
reflectionQuestion: A situation where more for them automatically felt like less for you?
whyHumansDoThis: Competition trains us to see wins as taking slices from the same pie.
learningObjective: Learn to look for growing or shared wins before assuming trade-offs.

statement: My sibling's happy relationship somehow felt like a comment on mine — as if happiness were limited.
explanation: Their good news was read as reducing what was available to me.
reflectionQuestion: Someone else's good news that quietly felt threatening to you?
whyHumansDoThis: When we feel scarce in an area, other people's gains can feel like our losses.
learningObjective: Learn that many good outcomes can coexist without cancelling each other.

statement: Two drivers rolled through the same stop sign — one got fined, one didn't. We called one reckless and one fine.
explanation: The same choice got different moral labels because the outcomes differed.
reflectionQuestion: When has luck changed how harshly you judged the same kind of choice?
whyHumansDoThis: Outcomes are vivid, so we often grade the decision by how lucky the ending was.
learningObjective: Learn to judge the decision separately from how lucky the result was.

statement: The risky idea that worked made her a genius; the same idea that failed made him careless.
explanation: Moral judgment followed the outcome, not just the risk taken.
reflectionQuestion: A bold move praised in one person but criticised in another because results differed?
whyHumansDoThis: Success and failure are memorable endings, so they can rewrite how brave a choice looked.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate risk-taking by what was known at the time, not only by what happened.

statement: A joke landed well in one room and offended in another — and we judged the teller's character differently each time.
explanation: The same action got different moral weight because outcomes differed.
reflectionQuestion: An action you judged more harshly because it happened to go badly?
whyHumansDoThis: Harm that actually happened feels more blameworthy than the same gamble that luckily did no harm.
learningObjective: Learn to separate intent and risk from the luck of how it landed.

statement: After the fact, we pointed to three wins as proof of our strategy — ignoring all the misses.
explanation: The pattern was drawn around the hits after they happened.
reflectionQuestion: A success story built by cherry-picking the examples that fit?
whyHumansDoThis: Clusters stand out after the fact, and it is easy to pretend we aimed at them from the start.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what you predicted before the hits, not only what you notice afterward.

statement: All the best cafés seem to be on this street — but we only counted the good ones, not the weak ones nearby.
explanation: A target was drawn around the positives after looking around.
reflectionQuestion: A pattern you noticed only after choosing where to look?
whyHumansDoThis: Once we see a clump, it is tempting to treat that clump as the whole map.
learningObjective: Learn to count the misses in the same area before declaring a pattern.

statement: The wellness trend 'works' because we highlight the people who improved and forget everyone who didn't.
explanation: Proof was built by circling the winners after the results were known.
reflectionQuestion: A trend praised using only the success stories you went looking for?
whyHumansDoThis: After results arrive, it is easy to draw a neat circle around the cases that support our belief.
learningObjective: Learn to test claims on the full group, not only the highlighted hits.

statement: I was 90% sure I knew the answer — and I was wrong.
explanation: Certainty ran ahead of actual accuracy.
reflectionQuestion: A time you felt completely sure and still missed?
whyHumansDoThis: Confidence is a feeling, not a scorecard — but it often feels like proof.
learningObjective: Learn to treat strong certainty as a signal to double-check, not to stop checking.

statement: We estimated the project would definitely finish early — no one built in serious delay.
explanation: The team sounded more certain than past results justified.
reflectionQuestion: A plan where confidence was high but evidence for it was thin?
whyHumansDoThis: Groups can amplify confidence because shared certainty feels reassuring.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what could make your confident forecast wrong.

statement: I only studied the questions I was sure about — and was surprised by the ones I 'knew' I'd get right.
explanation: High confidence led to skipping the very areas that needed review.
reflectionQuestion: Topics you skipped revising because you already felt certain?
whyHumansDoThis: Feeling sure can shut down the extra practice that unsure topics still get.
learningObjective: Learn to study the questions you feel safest about, not only the shaky ones.

statement: We bought the shiny new software before asking whether the old system was actually the problem.
explanation: Newness itself was treated as a reason to switch.
reflectionQuestion: A new tool adopted because it felt modern, not because it solved the issue?
whyHumansDoThis: Novel solutions carry hope, and hope can skip the boring step of diagnosing the real problem.
learningObjective: Learn to ask what problem a new tool solves before buying the promise of new.

statement: The hospital rolled out a new app for booking — but the phones worked fine and fewer older patients could use it.
explanation: Innovation was assumed helpful before its limits were weighed.
reflectionQuestion: A digital change that helped some people but made things harder for others?
whyHumansDoThis: New can signal progress so strongly that trade-offs get less attention.
learningObjective: Learn to test who benefits and who loses before calling an innovation an upgrade.

statement: The school replaced a simple paper form with a complicated portal — because 'it's time to modernise.'
explanation: Modernising was treated as enough reason, without checking if it helped users.
reflectionQuestion: A process made more complex in the name of improvement?
whyHumansDoThis: Innovation can feel like leadership, even when the old way was clearer for most people.
learningObjective: Learn to judge changes by user outcomes, not by how advanced they look.

statement: The team dismissed the new safety checklist because 'we've never needed it before.'
explanation: New guidance was rejected because it challenged the old way.
reflectionQuestion: A new practice brushed off mainly because it was not how things were done?
whyHumansDoThis: Established routines feel tested, so unfamiliar fixes can feel wrong before they are tried.
learningObjective: Learn to judge new evidence on its merits, not on how long the old way lasted.

statement: Doctors resisted handwashing at first — the idea clashed too hard with what they already believed.
explanation: Evidence was rejected because it threatened established practice.
reflectionQuestion: A good idea you or others resisted because it upset the usual story?
whyHumansDoThis: When new evidence attacks a trusted norm, the norm can fight back harder than the data.
learningObjective: Learn to notice when rejection is about identity and tradition, not about the evidence itself.

statement: The office laughed off the intern's suggestion — then adopted the same idea when a senior manager repeated it.
explanation: The idea was rejected at first because it came from outside the established view.
reflectionQuestion: A useful suggestion ignored until someone more senior said it?
whyHumansDoThis: New ideas can threaten hierarchy and habit, so they get filtered through who is allowed to challenge norms.
learningObjective: Learn to evaluate suggestions by content, not only by how familiar or senior the source feels.

statement: After I got better brakes, I found myself driving faster in the rain.
explanation: Feeling safer led to taking more risk, partly offsetting the safety gain.
reflectionQuestion: A safety upgrade that made you act a bit bolder afterward?
whyHumansDoThis: When danger feels lower, we often give some of that margin back through looser behaviour.
learningObjective: Learn that feeling safer can quietly increase risk-taking.

statement: With the helmet on, I took a trickier line on my bike than I would have without it.
explanation: Protection made a riskier choice feel more acceptable.
reflectionQuestion: Safety gear that made you push a little harder than before?
whyHumansDoThis: Extra protection lowers the perceived cost of risk, so we sometimes spend that savings on more risk.
learningObjective: Learn to keep habits steady when safety equipment improves, instead of spending the buffer.

statement: The new handrails made the stairs feel safer — so I started texting while walking down them.
explanation: Added safety was partly cancelled by paying less attention.
reflectionQuestion: A safety feature that made you less careful in another way?
whyHumansDoThis: When the environment feels safer, attention can drift — and attention is its own safety layer.
learningObjective: Learn that safety improvements work best when behaviour does not loosen to match.
